speaker: interviewer
text: This is Susan Rapaport, R-A-P-A-P-O-R-T, June 24, 1996. And this is take two. Susan, you were telling us that, to a certain extent, the Jewish families were still able to celebrate the holidays. How did the celebrations change from what they were before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, we were afraid to go to synagogues already. And we could only go in the back streets. And services had to be cut short. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were glad to get home. We never knew what was happening-- what could happen to us, because sometimes many people, when they came out of the synagogue, was beaten. And things were rough, like I said. Not only-- I mean, actually the Germans weren't in yet, but from our own Hungarian guards. They were terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there police besides the Hungarian guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, the police was bad. And then there were, like, troopers. We called them gendarme. They were very anti-Semitic.

speaker: interviewer
text: What nationality were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hungarian.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel about this kind of anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: We felt very, very bad. They made us feel like second-class citizens. But we never gave up hope that one day this shall end. And I think that's what gave us the strength to go on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Judenrat-- there was, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they help the Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not-- not much, really. Matter of fact, almost nothing. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The only thing they did-- I don't even know if they did anything. One thing I remember positively-- when they came and got some of the leaders of the Jewish community together. And I believe this was already for the order of the Germans. And they got them together in the synagogue. But actually, this was when they started already the ghetto, when all the people had to go into the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you went into the ghetto, were the Jews still able to leave the town if they wanted?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't know. I think it was very difficult. No. They used to take off Jews from the trains and bring them in to so-called ghettos. Actually, in my town was the main ghetto. And they brought from all over the small villages the Jews in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get any warning that you would be moving into the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. They just came and said, get your things together, and we go. This is how it was for the other people. But for my father, my brother, and me, we were not moved into the ghetto-- only much later. So we knew that the ghetto existed. But because of my father was privileged, for the beginning, we did not have to go into the ghetto. But then, of course, one day they came and said, let's go. We were limited, very limited what to take in. We had to leave our home. And we were also taken into the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to take your valuable possessions with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. Only personal things, very little. And then, one day, they came for my father. And they said they have to take him in. And they came to all the people who were leaders and somebodies in the city. I assume the Germans wanted them to keep them for ransom. By that time, the synagogues also were part of the ghetto. And that's where they got them together.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did that go on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. [SIGHS] Not too long. About [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] two weeks. I really can't remember exactly, but something like that-- a couple of weeks. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Maybe a few days, more or less. I-- I-- actually, that was such a horrible time that I really can't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they receive their ransom then?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Obviously not, because we were the first transport to take us to Auschwitz-Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't get the ransom. Then what happened to your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was waiting there until we were taken into the wagons.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- were you able to keep in touch with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We were able to go in there. And matter of fact, where our synagogue was, next to it was the Jewish school. And they fixed it up as a temporary hospital that the people who got sick in the ghetto were taken there. And some of our Jewish doctors volunteered to help them, and some of the women also. I was one of them who volunteered to help, to be as a nurse. And that was a very sad time because we did not get medicine-- very limited. Everything was very, very limited. The only thing we could do-- perhaps to keep them clean and make them feel a little better.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you notified that you would be leaving the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. They just came, the gendarme, the Hungarians. And they said we have to leave. They're taking us to work. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there are trains waiting for us. And we should get our personal belongings. And we did. My father was able to come back for us. And we brought some of our personal belongings. We went to the railroad tracks. And there we saw these cattle wagons and Germans in front of them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They kept telling us, oh, don't worry. You're going to work. And you will have everything what you need. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had no choice because they were pushing us into these wagons. We hardly had any room to breathe. We just couldn't hardly sit down on the floor. It was packed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were very fearful. We didn't know where they were taking us. We didn't know what's going to happen. And-- [SIGHS] --we got no drinks. We had some food with us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Oh, after about two days, they let us, one by one, to relieve themselves and pushed them back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you saying you were not allowed out for two days?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened during those two days? Were there sanitary facilities on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was sad. Either you held yourself back, you got sick, or just relieved yourself. It was terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family together on this train? Who was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, my brother, and I. The rest of the relatives were left at home. We were the first one to be taken. We didn't know what'll happen to them or where are we going at that point.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the reaction of the Christian people when they saw the Jews being taken away like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not much. They were happy to go and raid our homes and take away our things, our valuables. Nobody felt sorry. Absolutely nobody tried to help.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever contacted by some of your ex-friends, Christian friends from school or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --from the area?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Basically, the Hungarian people were happy. They had free robbery.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the time you were taken on these trains, were you aware of the truth about the extermination camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: No way. No, we never heard of it. And we didn't know where we were going. And we just didn't think we're gonna be killed, obviously. Everybody would run different directions, even if they would shoot us. No, we believed them that we are going to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were taken and your father was with you, did he attempt to explain to some of the gendarmes that he was a veteran?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was absolutely nothing to say. You opened your mouth, you got slapped. They treated us like animals.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was the train ride?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was four days, if I remember good. Approximately four days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a chance to get water during the ride at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nothing. A few little things what we had with us, and that was all, but no water.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did the train stop?

speaker: interviewee
text: The train stopped-- what we found out later-- in Auschwitz. We had no idea what Auschwitz was. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We just saw German soldiers outside. And they opened the wagons. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we saw people in striped clothes. They spoke to us Jewish. And they said to us in Jewish, 'Go to work. Go to work,' very quietly. And as we came off, we saw a German soldier, whoever-- we didn't know who it was-- with two others. And he picked up his thumb and was going left and right, left and right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what was it that he picked up?

speaker: interviewee
text: His thumb. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was terribly, terribly thirsty. And I asked one of these boys who took us off the train, please give me a little water. And the only thing I had with me-- my mother had a string of pearls. That was her favorite. She always wore it. I took it with me. And I gave it to him he should give me a cup of water. And he brought it for me. [SIGHS] As we were getting off, my father and brother had to go one side, and I had to go to the other side. I saw they were separating the men from the women. But then, also, they were separating some other people, older people and children. Well, we didn't know what was happening. We were all panicky. But nobody dared open their mouth, because it was pushed and kicked by those three soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father have an opportunity to say anything to you before you were sent in different directions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, very little. The only thing he said, 'Take care. Watch yourself. I'll see you later.' That's what he thought. And he took my brother with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell where they were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not at that point.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which way did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] We went to the right of the wagon [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as we were taken off. Then the men went to their left. And there was a whole big [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] transport. There were lots of people who sort of went, like, in the middle. In the middle, there were these truck-- trucks, like wagon cars, waiting for them. And the older people and the children were put on that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we thought perhaps, you know, they shouldn't be walking. It would be hard for them. Wherever they put me, there were all young people. And I figured, well, we perhaps could walk wherever we have to go. That's why we are on this side and they were put up in these trucks and left. What was the date that you arrived at Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] That's very difficult to say exactly. But it was-- I can't tell you the day. But it was the last week of April, or maybe the first days of May.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you arrive during the daytime or at night?

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived during the day. Yes, during the daytime.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see any signs or anything to tell you where you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, except it was written, 'Auschwitz,' at the station. But we didn't know what it meant. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then we had to march, oh, maybe three quarters of an hour or something. I don't know exactly. And then there was a sign, 'Birkenau,' which we also didn't know what it was. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: But we figured that's where we're coming to work. And what happened after you arrived at Birkenau?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] When we arrived to Birkenau, we had SS all around us with dogs, big German shepherds. And they put us into a room [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and said, you have to get undressed. Leave your belongings. You will get everything back later.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we did that. And then they took us into a shower. Of course, no towels or anything like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they took us into another room where there were some girls who shaved our heads. And then they gave us these [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] dresses to put on, which was a piece of rag, actually. And it had-- on our back, there was a-- a red stripe. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And once we got all this, we were marched into-- we had to go outside-- and five in a row. And there was a girl, who later I found out was Czech, who was called the lager-- the blockalteste. And that meant she was responsible for that barrack where we were taken in. She took over and marched us into this barrack where there were [? pritches ?] for beds-- three, one above the other. And there were five people put in there, five of us in one row, in one [? pritch. ?] So actually, in each row, there were 15 people. And we used to sleep one head, one foot, one head, one foot. [SIGHS] And when we started asking questions, they said, don't ask any questions.

speaker: interviewer
text: You want to know where your parents are, where your relatives are? Look out. You see the flame? Do you smell this smell? That's where they are burning.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were shocked. We didn't know. We were absolutely numb. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We just had to lay in those beds. And every morning, about 5 o'clock, we had to get up. And 6 o'clock, we had to stand in line outside, which was called 'saal appel.' That meant they had to count us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After they counted us, which was maybe for about an hour, we went back to our so-called-- the barrack. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then they gave us for breakfast a so-called-- it was called coffee. But it was more like dirty dishwater. We had to drink that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then, every night, we we're getting a small piece of bread, which had to be divided into five. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] If you looked real good, you could look through the slices. That's how thin they were. And that was our food for the day. Once a week, we used to get a very, very thin slice of wurst, which was like a salami, but very thin, or a very, very small piece of cheese. But otherwise, during the whole week, we would get this piece of bread, which was sliced into five, and this so-called coffee.

speaker: interviewer
text: Obviously, they must have put something into the coffee-- which later we found out it was true-- which calmed us and made us sort of a-- most people-- made those, like, couldn't care, you know? Numb. Certain numbness.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were some of us who was crying 24 hours. There were some of us who got hysterical, so they were beaten and put back to lay there. And this went on, I think, for about four weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Coming back to when you first arrived, was there a registration?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not yet. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you receive a number at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not then. Later.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that you were given-- your clothes were taken away. Were you able to keep your shoes?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the only thing we were able to keep, yes. That was the only thing. So we were actually naked with our shoes on and no hair. Lucky there were no mirrors. Only we saw each other, and we could imagine what we looked like.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with anyone that you knew, anyone from your hometown?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, I had a few girls, sure. We all left together. At that point, yes, which [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] lasted till about four to five weeks, like I said. And then we were called out in the middle of the day for a saal appel, a lineup. We didn't know what it was. But our [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Czech girl told us, and everybody told us, if they come to choose somebody for work, we should try to go. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As we were standing there, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] two SS came, looked us over, and about less than a half of the people who were standing in line-- motioned with his hand, cut off one part and put on one side. And then there was the other side. And he cut off exactly by my row. And I had the feeling that the smaller portion, whom he cut off, must have been chosen for work. And one of my friends were there also. And as he cut off, I ran to the other side-- I tried to run to the other side. And the SS got a-- caught me and beat me up terrible. But as he was beating and kicking me, I fell to the working side, just by accident. And then, of course, the girls pulled me into them. And sure enough, those girls were chosen for work.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you think that you knew which was the side you should be on?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know it. The only thing we knew that-- that-- the only thing-- I saw that that was a smaller portion. And also, I had a couple people with whom I was together. I wanted to get there. I didn't expect him to beat me. But he beat me, very luckily, that I fell to the working side. And that was the transport-- the few girls who had to go in and be tattooed.

speaker: interviewer
text: We will pause now to change the film.
